```{r}
# Calculate number of markers for dynamic plot sizing
n_markers <- length(marker_order)

# Calculate plot dimensions based on number of markers
# Base size + scaling factor to ensure readability across different marker counts
# Minimum size ensures small marker sets are still readable, scaling factor accommodates larger sets
base_width <- 6
base_height <- 6
marker_scaling_factor <- 0.15 # Additional inches per marker
plot_width <- max(base_width, base_width + (n_markers - 20) * marker_scaling_factor)
plot_height <- max(base_height, base_height + (n_markers - 20) * marker_scaling_factor)

# Calculate global scale ranges across all samples
# This ensures all heatmaps have consistent color and size scales for fair comparison
global_value_range <- range(summary_proximity_data$mean_log2_ratio, na.rm = TRUE)
global_size_range <- range(summary_proximity_data$pct_detected, na.rm = TRUE)

# Use symmetric range for value scale (centered around 0) to match legend_range behavior
# But ensure it covers the actual data range
max_abs_value <- max(abs(global_value_range))
global_value_limits <- c(-max_abs_value, max_abs_value)

# Get all unique sample_alias values
unique_samples <- unique(summary_proximity_data$sample_alias)

# Create heatmaps for each sample using the reference marker order
# Store plots in a list to combine them later
sample_heatmaps <- list()

for (sample in unique_samples) {
    # Filter data for current sample
    sample_data <- summary_proximity_data |> filter(sample_alias == sample)

    tmp_plot <- ColocalizationHeatmap(
        sample_data,
        marker1_col = "marker_1",
        marker2_col = "marker_2",
        value_col = "mean_log2_ratio",
        size_col = "pct_detected",
        type = "dots",
        cluster_cols = FALSE,
        cluster_rows = FALSE,
        legend_range = global_value_limits # Use global range for consistent scaling
    ) +
        ggtitle(glue::glue("Raw colocalization heatmap for {sample}"))

    # Apply the reference marker order to the ggplot object by modifying the scales
    # This ensures all heatmaps have consistent marker ordering
    tmp_plot <- tmp_plot +
        scale_x_discrete(limits = marker_order, drop = FALSE, position = "top") +
        scale_y_discrete(limits = rev(marker_order), drop = FALSE)

    # Ensure consistent scales across all samples for fair comparison
    # The legend_range parameter already handles the value scale, but we need to ensure
    # the size scale is also consistent. Try both fill and color aesthetics in case
    # ColocalizationHeatmap uses either one
    plot_layers <- tmp_plot$layers
    has_fill <- any(sapply(plot_layers, function(l) "fill" %in% names(l$mapping) || "fill" %in% names(l$aes_params)))
    has_color <- any(sapply(plot_layers, function(l) "colour" %in% names(l$mapping) || "color" %in% names(l$mapping) || "colour" %in% names(l$aes_params)))

    # Apply value scale limits (legend_range should handle this, but ensure it's applied)
    if (has_fill) {
        tmp_plot <- tmp_plot + scale_fill_continuous(limits = global_value_limits, oob = scales::oob_squish)
    }
    if (has_color && !has_fill) {
        tmp_plot <- tmp_plot + scale_color_continuous(limits = global_value_limits, oob = scales::oob_squish)
    }

    # Ensure consistent size scale across all samples
    tmp_plot <- tmp_plot + scale_size_continuous(limits = global_size_range, range = c(0.2, 3))

    # Save individual plot with dimensions scaled to number of markers
    save_plot(
        filename = glue::glue(here::here("results/04_raw_proximity/raw_colocalization_heatmap_per_sample/{sample}_raw_colocalization_heatmap")),
        plot = tmp_plot,
        width = plot_width,
        height = plot_height,
        create_dir = TRUE
    )

    # Store plot for combined visualization
    sample_heatmaps[[sample]] <- tmp_plot
}

# Create a combined plot with all samples side by side
# Use patchwork to combine plots (install with: install.packages("patchwork"))
n_samples <- length(unique_samples)
n_rows <- 2
n_cols <- ceiling(n_samples / n_rows)
combined_plot <- patchwork::wrap_plots(sample_heatmaps, nrow = n_rows)

# Calculate combined plot dimensions: scale width by number of columns, height by number of rows
# Each subplot maintains its aspect ratio while accommodating multiple samples
combined_width <- plot_width * n_cols
combined_height <- plot_height * n_rows

# Save the combined plot
save_plot(
    filename = glue::glue(here::here("results/04_raw_proximity/raw_colocalization_heatmap_per_sample/all_samples_combined_raw_colocalization_heatmap")),
    plot = combined_plot,
    width = combined_width,
    height = combined_height,
    create_dir = TRUE
)
```

### 4.1.2. Per cluster

This section generates cell type-specific colocalization heatmaps, analyzing colocalization patterns separately for each annotated cell type. For each cell type, colocalization heatmaps are created using a set of markers relevant to that specific cell type (defined in `markers_to_test`). The analysis is performed on cells belonging to each cell type separately, then heatmaps are generated for each sample within that cell type. This enables identification of cell type-specific colocalization patterns and comparison of how these patterns vary across samples within the same cell type.

The visualization follows the same principles as the per-sample analysis: color intensity represents the mean log2 ratio of observed versus expected colocalization, and dot size indicates the percentage of cells in which the marker pair was detected. All heatmaps for a given cell type use consistent marker ordering and scales to enable direct comparison across samples. Individual heatmaps are saved for each sample-cell type combination, and combined visualizations showing all samples side-by-side are generated for each cell type. Results are organized in separate subdirectories for each cell type to facilitate navigation and interpretation.

```{r}
#| label: colocalization_raw_heatmap_per_cell_type

cell_types <- unique(pg_data[[]]$cell_annotation)

for (cell_type in cell_types) {
    expressed_markers <- markers_to_test[[cell_type]]
    pg_data_cell_type <- subset(pg_data, cell_annotation == cell_type)

    proximity_data <- ProximityScores(pg_data_cell_type,
        lazy = TRUE,
        meta_data_columns = c("sample_alias")
    ) |>
        filter(marker_1 %in% expressed_markers) |>
        filter(marker_2 %in% expressed_markers) |>
        collect()

    summary_proximity_data <- SummarizeProximityScores(proximity_data, group_vars = "sample_alias")
    rm(proximity_data)
    gc()

    # Create reference heatmap to determine marker ordering
    reference_heatmap <- ColocalizationHeatmap(
        summary_proximity_data |> filter(sample_alias == "S1_resting"),
        marker1_col = "marker_1",
        marker2_col = "marker_2",
        value_col = "mean_log2_ratio",
        size_col = "pct_detected",
        type = "dots",
        cluster_cols = TRUE,
        cluster_rows = TRUE,
        legend_range = c(-1, 1)
    )

    marker_order <- layer_scales(reference_heatmap)$y$range$range

    # Calculate number of markers for dynamic plot sizing
    n_markers <- length(marker_order)

    # Calculate plot dimensions based on number of markers
    # Base size + scaling factor to ensure readability across different marker counts
    # Minimum size ensures small marker sets are still readable, scaling factor accommodates larger sets
    base_width <- 6
    base_height <- 6
    marker_scaling_factor <- 0.15 # Additional inches per marker
    plot_width <- max(base_width, base_width + (n_markers - 20) * marker_scaling_factor)
    plot_height <- max(base_height, base_height + (n_markers - 20) * marker_scaling_factor)

    # Calculate global scale ranges across all samples for this cell type
    # This ensures all heatmaps have consistent color and size scales for fair comparison
    global_value_range <- range(summary_proximity_data$mean_log2_ratio, na.rm = TRUE)
    global_size_range <- range(summary_proximity_data$pct_detected, na.rm = TRUE)

    # Use symmetric range for value scale (centered around 0) to match legend_range behavior
    # But ensure it covers the actual data range
    max_abs_value <- max(abs(global_value_range))
    global_value_limits <- c(-max_abs_value, max_abs_value)

    # Get all unique sample_alias values
    unique_samples <- unique(summary_proximity_data$sample_alias)

    # Create heatmaps for each sample using the reference marker order
    # Store plots in a list to combine them later
    sample_heatmaps <- list()

    for (sample in unique_samples) {
        # Filter data for current sample
        sample_data <- summary_proximity_data |> filter(sample_alias == sample)

        tmp_plot <- ColocalizationHeatmap(
            sample_data,
            marker1_col = "marker_1",
            marker2_col = "marker_2",
            value_col = "mean_log2_ratio",
            size_col = "pct_detected",
            type = "dots",
            cluster_cols = FALSE,
            cluster_rows = FALSE,
            legend_range = global_value_limits # Use global range for consistent scaling
        ) +
            ggtitle(glue::glue("Raw colocalization heatmap for {cell_type} in {sample}"))

        # Apply the reference marker order to the ggplot object by modifying the scales
        # This ensures all heatmaps have consistent marker ordering
        tmp_plot <- tmp_plot +
            scale_x_discrete(limits = marker_order, drop = FALSE, position = "top") +
            scale_y_discrete(limits = rev(marker_order), drop = FALSE)

        # Ensure consistent scales across all samples for fair comparison
        # The legend_range parameter already handles the value scale, but we need to ensure
        # the size scale is also consistent. Try both fill and color aesthetics in case
        # ColocalizationHeatmap uses either one
        plot_layers <- tmp_plot$layers
        has_fill <- any(sapply(plot_layers, function(l) "fill" %in% names(l$mapping) || "fill" %in% names(l$aes_params)))
        has_color <- any(sapply(plot_layers, function(l) "colour" %in% names(l$mapping) || "color" %in% names(l$mapping) || "colour" %in% names(l$aes_params)))

        # Apply value scale limits (legend_range should handle this, but ensure it's applied)
        if (has_fill) {
            tmp_plot <- tmp_plot + scale_fill_continuous(limits = global_value_limits, oob = scales::oob_squish)
        }
        if (has_color && !has_fill) {
            tmp_plot <- tmp_plot + scale_color_continuous(limits = global_value_limits, oob = scales::oob_squish)
        }

        # Ensure consistent size scale across all samples
        tmp_plot <- tmp_plot + scale_size_continuous(limits = global_size_range, range = c(0.5, 5))

        # Save individual plot with dimensions scaled to number of markers
        save_plot(
            filename = glue::glue(here::here("results/04_raw_proximity/raw_colocalization_heatmap_per_cell_type/{cell_type}/{sample}_{cell_type}_raw_colocalization_heatmap")),
            plot = tmp_plot,
            width = plot_width,
            height = plot_height,
            create_dir = TRUE
        )

        # Store plot for combined visualization
        sample_heatmaps[[sample]] <- tmp_plot
    }

    # Create a combined plot with all samples side by side
    # Use patchwork to combine plots (install with: install.packages("patchwork"))
    n_samples <- length(unique_samples)
    n_rows <- 2
    n_cols <- ceiling(n_samples / n_rows)
    combined_plot <- patchwork::wrap_plots(sample_heatmaps, nrow = n_rows)

    # Calculate combined plot dimensions: scale width by number of columns, height by number of rows
    # Each subplot maintains its aspect ratio while accommodating multiple samples
    combined_width <- plot_width * n_cols
    combined_height <- plot_height * n_rows

    # Save the combined plot
    save_plot(
        filename = glue::glue(here::here("results/04_raw_proximity/raw_colocalization_heatmap_per_cell_type/{cell_type}/{cell_type}_all_samples_combined_raw_colocalization_heatmap")),
        plot = combined_plot,
        width = combined_width,
        height = combined_height,
        create_dir = TRUE
    )
}
```


## 4.2. Colocalization Score network

Network visualizations provide an alternative representation of colocalization patterns that can reveal complex relationships and connectivity structures between markers. Unlike heatmaps, which display all pairwise interactions in a matrix format, network graphs focus on the most significant colocalization relationships by filtering based on statistical and biological criteria. This filtering approach helps reduce visual clutter and highlights the most biologically relevant interactions.

In network visualizations, markers are represented as nodes, and colocalization relationships are shown as edges connecting pairs of markers. Edge color encodes the strength of colocalization (mean log2 ratio), while edge width represents the magnitude of the colocalization effect. The network layout algorithm positions nodes to minimize edge crossings and optimize visual clarity, with markers that share similar colocalization patterns tending to cluster together spatially.

The network is constructed by filtering marker pairs based on two key criteria: (1) a minimum colocalization threshold (`metric_threshold`), which can be applied to either positive colocalization only or absolute values, and (2) a minimum detection threshold (`pct_detected_threshold`), ensuring that only marker pairs detected in a sufficient proportion of cells are included. These filters help focus the visualization on robust, biologically meaningful colocalization relationships while reducing noise from weak or rare interactions.

This section provides two complementary analyses: a whole-dataset network visualization that captures global colocalization patterns across all cell types, and cell type-specific network visualizations that reveal how colocalization patterns differ between cell types. Users should select a focused set of markers of interest (`network_markers`) for network visualization, as including too many markers can make the network difficult to interpret.

### 4.2.1. All Cell Types

This section generates network visualizations that capture colocalization patterns across all cell types in the dataset, with separate network plots created for each sample. This whole-dataset approach provides a global view of marker colocalization relationships and enables comparison of how these relationships vary across samples. The analysis includes all cells regardless of cell type annotation, making it useful for identifying sample-wide colocalization patterns and detecting potential batch effects or experimental condition-specific changes.

Users should specify a focused set of `network_markers` for visualization, as network graphs become difficult to interpret with too many nodes. The filtering thresholds (`metric_threshold` and `pct_detected_threshold`) help focus the visualization on the most robust colocalization relationships. The `only_positive_mean_log2_ratio` parameter can be set to filter for only positive colocalization (markers that co-occur more frequently than expected) or to include both positive and negative colocalization relationships.

```{r}
#| label: colocalization_score_network_all_cell_types

network_markers <- c("CD52", "CD59", "CD81", "CD82", "CD43", "CD44", "CD45", "CD45RA", "CD45RB", "CD8")
only_positive_mean_log2_ratio <- TRUE
# Define the threshold for the metric and the percentage of detected cells - this will be used to filter the data for the network plot
metric_threshold <- 0.1
pct_detected_threshold <- 0.5

proximity_data <- ProximityScores(pg_data,
    lazy = TRUE,
    meta_data_columns = c("sample_alias")
) |>
    filter(marker_1 %in% network_markers) |>
    filter(marker_2 %in% network_markers) |>
    collect()

summary_proximity_data <- SummarizeProximityScores(proximity_data, group_vars = "sample_alias")
rm(proximity_data)
gc()

# Create the network plot: filter the data for the network plot based on the metric and the percentage of detected cells, then create the network plot - this is also where you can include additional filtering options, such as for example to only include positive mean_log2_ratio.
df_net <- summary_proximity_data %>%
    filter(if (only_positive_mean_log2_ratio) mean_log2_ratio > metric_threshold else abs(mean_log2_ratio) > metric_threshold) %>%
    filter(pct_detected > pct_detected_threshold) %>%
    mutate(from = marker_1, to = marker_2) %>%
    select(from, to, mean_log2_ratio, sample_alias)

network_plot_all_cell_types <- tidygraph::as_tbl_graph(df_net) %>%
    ggraph(layout = "kk") +
    geom_edge_link(aes(color = mean_log2_ratio, width = abs(mean_log2_ratio))) +
    geom_node_label(aes(label = name), size = 2.5) +
    scale_edge_color_gradientn(
        colours = c(
            "#1F395F", "#647DA3", "#BBC8DA",
            "#FFFFFF", "#F0BCCD", "#BE5F7D",
            "#781534"
        ),
        limits = c(-abs(max(df_net$mean_log2_ratio)), abs(max(df_net$mean_log2_ratio)))
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0.2, 0.2)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0.2, 0.2)) +
    facet_edges(~sample_alias, nrow = 2) +
    theme(
        aspect.ratio = 1,
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black"),
        panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
    ) +
    labs(edge_color = "Mean\nlog2 ratio", title = "Colocalization Score Network All Cell Types", subtitle = glue::glue("Metric threshold{if (only_positive_mean_log2_ratio) ' (only positive)' else ' (absolute value)'}: {metric_threshold}, Percentage of cells above threshold: {pct_detected_threshold}")) +
    guides(edge_width = "none")

save_plot(
    filename = glue::glue(here::here("results/04_raw_proximity/colocalization_score_network/colocalization_score_network_all_cell_types/all_cell_types_colocalization_score_network")),
    plot = network_plot_all_cell_types,
    width = 12,
    height = 8,
    create_dir = TRUE
)

```


### 4.2.2. Per Cell Type

This section generates cell type-specific network visualizations, analyzing colocalization patterns within a single cell type of interest. By focusing on a specific cell type, this analysis can reveal cell type-specific colocalization signatures that may be masked in whole-dataset analyses. The network visualization helps identify which markers form strong colocalization relationships within the selected cell type and how these relationships vary across samples.

Users should specify the `cell_type_of_interest` variable to select which cell type to analyze. The same filtering criteria and visualization principles apply as in the all-cell-types analysis, but the proximity data is filtered to include only cells belonging to the specified cell type. This enables identification of cell type-specific marker interactions and comparison of how colocalization networks differ between cell types.

```{r}
#| label: colocalization_score_network_per_cell_type

network_markers <- c("CD52", "CD59", "CD81", "CD82", "CD43", "CD44", "CD45", "CD45RA", "CD45RB", "CD8")
cell_type_of_interest <- "CD8 T"
only_positive_mean_log2_ratio <- TRUE
# Define the threshold for the metric and the percentage of detected cells - this will be used to filter the data for the network plot
metric_threshold <- 0.1
pct_detected_threshold <- 0.5

proximity_data <- ProximityScores(pg_data,
    lazy = TRUE,
    meta_data_columns = c("sample_alias", "cell_annotation")
) |>
    filter(cell_annotation == cell_type_of_interest) |>
    filter(marker_1 %in% network_markers) |>
    filter(marker_2 %in% network_markers) |>
    filter(marker_1 != marker_2) |>
    collect()

summary_proximity_data <- SummarizeProximityScores(proximity_data, group_vars = "sample_alias")
rm(proximity_data)
gc()

# Create the network plot: filter the data for the network plot based on the metric and the percentage of detected cells, then create the network plot - this is also where you can include additional filtering options, such as for example to only include positive mean_log2_ratio.
df_net <- summary_proximity_data %>%
    filter(if (only_positive_mean_log2_ratio) mean_log2_ratio > metric_threshold else abs(mean_log2_ratio) > metric_threshold) %>%
    filter(pct_detected > pct_detected_threshold) %>%
    mutate(from = marker_1, to = marker_2) %>%
    select(from, to, mean_log2_ratio, sample_alias)

network_plot_cell_type_of_interest <- tidygraph::as_tbl_graph(df_net) %>%
    ggraph(layout = "kk") +
    geom_edge_link(aes(color = mean_log2_ratio, width = abs(mean_log2_ratio))) +
    geom_node_label(aes(label = name), size = 2.5) +
    scale_edge_color_gradientn(
        colours = c(
            "#1F395F", "#647DA3", "#BBC8DA",
            "#FFFFFF", "#F0BCCD", "#BE5F7D",
            "#781534"
        ),
        limits = c(-abs(max(df_net$mean_log2_ratio)), abs(max(df_net$mean_log2_ratio)))
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0.2, 0.2)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0.2, 0.2)) +
    facet_edges(~sample_alias, nrow = 2) +
    theme(
        aspect.ratio = 1,
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = "black"),
        panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
    ) +
    labs(
        edge_color = "Mean\nlog2 ratio",
        title = glue::glue("Colocalization Score Network {cell_type_of_interest}"),
        subtitle = glue::glue(
            "Metric threshold{if (only_positive_mean_log2_ratio) ' (only positive)' else ' (absolute value)'}: {metric_threshold}, Percentage of cells above threshold: {pct_detected_threshold}"
        )
    ) +
    guides(edge_width = "none")

save_plot(
    filename = glue::glue(here::here("results/04_raw_proximity/colocalization_score_network/colocalization_score_network_per_cell_type/{cell_type_of_interest}/{cell_type_of_interest}_colocalization_score_network")),
    plot = network_plot_cell_type_of_interest,
    width = 12,
    height = 8,
    create_dir = TRUE
)

```




# 5. Statistical testing

## 5.1. Marker selection

```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_marker_selection


```



## 5.2. Differential abundance analysis

Differential abundance analysis identifies markers that exhibit statistically significant differences in expression levels between experimental conditions. This analysis is essential for understanding how experimental treatments, disease states, or other conditions affect protein expression patterns within specific cell types.

The analysis is performed separately for each annotated cell type, comparing marker abundance between a target condition (e.g., stimulated, treated) and a reference condition (e.g., resting, control). For each marker within each cell type, a statistical test (typically the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, also known as the Mann-Whitney U test) is used to assess whether the distribution of marker abundance differs significantly between conditions. The Wilcoxon test is a non-parametric test that does not assume normal distribution of the data, making it robust for single-cell data which often exhibits non-normal distributions and high variability.

The test compares marker expression values (typically CLR-normalized values) between cells in the target condition versus cells in the reference condition. For each marker, the analysis reports an effect size (typically the difference in mean or median expression) and a p-value indicating the statistical significance of the difference. Multiple testing correction is applied using the false discovery rate (FDR) method to control for the large number of statistical tests performed across multiple markers and cell types.

Results are organized by cell type and include both the raw statistical outputs and summary visualizations. Markers with significant differences (typically p_adj < 0.05) and large effect sizes are of particular biological interest, as they represent markers whose expression is substantially altered by the experimental condition. This analysis enables identification of condition-specific markers, understanding of cellular responses to treatments, and discovery of biomarkers associated with different experimental states.

```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_differential_abundance

## REMOVE THIS CHUNK AFTER TESTING

# Select 10 random markers to test for each cell type [TESTING]
markers_to_test <- lapply(unique(pg_data[[]]$cell_annotation), function(x) {
    sample(rownames(pg_data), size = 10)
})
names(markers_to_test) <- unique(pg_data[[]]$cell_annotation)

# create an additional condition factor to test multiple comparisons
pg_data$condition <- ifelse(pg_data$sample_alias == "S6_PHA", "PMA", pg_data$condition)
```


```{r}

differential_abundance_analysis <-
    names(markers_to_test) |>
    map(function(x) {
        tryCatch(
            {
                daa_res <- RunDAA(subset(pg_data, cell_annotation == x),
                    contrast_column = "condition",
                    reference = "resting",
                    features = markers_to_test[[x]]
                )
                daa_res$group <- x
                return(daa_res)
            },
            error = function(e) {
                message(paste("Error for cell type:", x, ":", e))
                return(NULL)
            }
        )
    }) |>
    bind_rows() |>
    mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(p, method = "fdr")) # recalculate the adjusted p values after multiple testing correction for all comparisons, not only per cell type

dir.create(here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/abundance"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Save the differential abundance analysis results to a file
saveRDS(differential_abundance_analysis, here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/abundance/differential_abundance_analysis.rds"))

# Save the differential abundance analysis results to a csv file
write.csv(differential_abundance_analysis, here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/abundance/differential_abundance_analysis.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

```    

### 5.2.1. Summarizing plots

Here we create two types of summarizing plots for the differential abundance analysis results:

1. **Volcano plot**: Shows the relationship between effect size (log fold change) and statistical significance (-log10 adjusted p-value) for each marker, faceted by cell type. This helps identify markers that are both significantly different and have large effect sizes.

2. **Heatmap**: Displays significant markers (adjusted p-value < 0.05) across all cell types, with colors representing the effect size. This provides an overview of which markers are differentially abundant in which cell types.

```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_differential_abundance_plots

# Determine the effect size column name
effect_size_col <- "difference"

# Determine the feature/marker column name
feature_col <- "marker"

# Extract target and reference condition names from the dataframe
# Handle multiple target conditions by identifying all comparisons
reference_condition <- "resting" # Known from RunDAA call

# Determine target conditions - check if dataframe has target/contrast columns
if ("target" %in% colnames(differential_abundance_analysis)) {
    # If target column exists, get all unique targets
    target_conditions <- unique(differential_abundance_analysis$target)
    target_conditions <- target_conditions[!is.na(target_conditions)]
} else {
    stop("Could not find target column. Available columns: ", paste(colnames(differential_abundance_analysis), collapse = ", "))
}

# Prepare base data for plotting (add target column if it doesn't exist)
plot_data_base <- differential_abundance_analysis |>
    mutate(
        neg_log10_p_adj = -log10(p_adj),
        significant = p_adj < 0.05,
        effect_size = !!sym(effect_size_col),
        marker = !!sym(feature_col)
    )


# Loop through each target condition and create plots
for (target_condition in target_conditions) {
    # Filter data for this target (if target column exists)
    if ("target" %in% colnames(plot_data_base)) {
        plot_data <- plot_data_base |>
            filter(target == target_condition)
    }

    # Skip if no data for this target
    if (nrow(plot_data) == 0) {
        warning(paste("No data found for target condition:", target_condition))
        next
    }

    # Create directory name based on target and reference
    comparison_dir <- paste0(target_condition, "_vs_", reference_condition)
    base_output_dir <- here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/differential_abundance_plots", comparison_dir)

    # Plot 1: Volcano plot showing effect size vs significance, faceted by cell type
    # This plot helps identify markers that are both significantly different and have large effect sizes
    # Identify top 5 most extreme effect sizes per cell type for labeling
    top_markers_faceted <- plot_data |>
        group_by(group) |>
        slice_max(order_by = abs(effect_size), n = 5) |>
        ungroup()

    volcano_plot_differential_abundance <- plot_data |>
        ggplot(aes(x = effect_size, y = neg_log10_p_adj, color = effect_size)) +
        geom_point() +
        scale_color_gradient2(
            low = "#0066FFFF",
            mid = "#74D055FF",
            high = "#FF0000FF",
            midpoint = 0
        ) +
        geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
        geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
        geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), col = "grey") +
        coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.1 * min(plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE), 1.1 * max(plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE))) +
        facet_wrap(~group, scales = "free") +
        geom_text(
            data = top_markers_faceted,
            aes(label = marker),
            hjust = -0.2,
            vjust = 0
        ) +
        theme_bw() +
        labs(
            title = paste("Differential abundance analysis: Volcano plot (", target_condition, " vs ", reference_condition, ")", sep = ""),
            subtitle = "Effect size vs statistical significance by cell type",
            x = paste("Effect size (", effect_size_col, ")", sep = ""),
            y = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
            color = "Effect size"
        ) +
        theme(
            strip.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue", color = "black"),
            panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
            panel.grid.major = element_blank()
        )

    # Save faceted volcano plot in the comparison directory
    save_plot(
        filename = file.path(base_output_dir, "differential_abundance_volcano"),
        plot = volcano_plot_differential_abundance,
        width = 12,
        height = 8,
        create_dir = TRUE
    )

    # Plot 2: Non-faceted volcano plots per cell type
    # Create individual folders per cell type and save non-faceted volcano plots for each cell type
    # Get unique cell types (groups)
    unique_cell_types <- unique(plot_data$group)

    # For each cell type, create a non-faceted volcano plot
    for (cell_type in unique_cell_types) {
        # Create directory for this cell type
        cell_type_dir <- file.path(base_output_dir, cell_type)
        dir.create(cell_type_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

        # Filter data for this cell type
        cell_type_plot_data <- plot_data |>
            filter(group == cell_type)

        # Create non-faceted volcano plot for this cell type
        # Identify top 5 most extreme effect sizes for labeling
        top_markers_cell_type <- cell_type_plot_data |>
            slice_max(order_by = abs(effect_size), n = 5)

        volcano_plot_cell_type <- cell_type_plot_data |>
            ggplot(aes(x = effect_size, y = neg_log10_p_adj, color = effect_size)) +
            geom_point() +
            scale_color_gradient2(
                low = "#0066FFFF",
                mid = "#74D055FF",
                high = "#FF0000FF",
                midpoint = 0
            ) +
            geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
            geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
            geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), col = "grey") +
            coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.1 * min(cell_type_plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE), 1.1 * max(cell_type_plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE))) +
            geom_text(
                data = top_markers_cell_type,
                aes(label = marker),
                hjust = -0.2,
                vjust = 0
            ) +
            theme_bw() +
            labs(
                title = paste("Differential abundance analysis: Volcano plot", sep = ""),
                subtitle = paste("Cell type: ", cell_type, " (", target_condition, " vs ", reference_condition, ")", sep = ""),
                x = paste("Effect size (", effect_size_col, ")", sep = ""),
                y = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
                color = "Effect size"
            ) +
            theme(
                plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
                plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
                panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
                panel.grid.major = element_blank()
            )

        # Save non-faceted volcano plot
        save_plot(
            filename = file.path(cell_type_dir, "volcano_plot"),
            plot = volcano_plot_cell_type,
            width = 8,
            height = 6,
            create_dir = FALSE # Directory already created
        )
    }

    # Plot 3: Heatmap showing significant markers across cell types
    # This provides an overview of which markers are differentially abundant in which cell types
    # Filter for significant markers and prepare data for heatmap
    significant_markers <- plot_data |>
        filter(significant) |>
        select(marker, group, effect_size) |>
        # Pivot to wide format for heatmap
        pivot_wider(names_from = group, values_from = effect_size, values_fill = 0) |>
        column_to_rownames("marker") |>
        as.matrix()

    # Only create heatmap if there are significant markers
    if (nrow(significant_markers) > 0 && ncol(significant_markers) > 0) {
        # Determine color scale breaks based on robust data range (use 1st and 99th percentiles to reduce outlier influence)
        effect_vals <- as.numeric(significant_markers)
        effect_quantiles <- quantile(effect_vals, probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
        max_abs_effect <- max(abs(effect_quantiles))
        breaks_range <- seq(-max_abs_effect, max_abs_effect, length.out = 101)

        heatmap_differential_abundance <- significant_markers |>
            pheatmap(
                color = PixelgenGradient(100, "BluesCherry"),
                breaks = breaks_range,
                cellwidth = 12,
                cellheight = 8,
                cluster_rows = TRUE,
                cluster_cols = TRUE,
                heatmap_legend_param = list(title = "Effect size"),
                fontsize = 8,
                fontsize_row = 6,
                fontsize_col = 8
            ) |>
            as.ggplot()

        save_plot(
            filename = file.path(base_output_dir, "differential_abundance_heatmap"),
            plot = heatmap_differential_abundance,
            width = 10,
            height = max(6, nrow(significant_markers) * 0.3),
            create_dir = TRUE
        )
    } else {
        warning(paste("No significant markers found for heatmap visualization (p_adj < 0.05) for", target_condition, "vs", reference_condition))
    }
}
```

## 5.3. Differential Clustering Analysis

Differential clustering analysis identifies markers that exhibit statistically significant differences in spatial clustering patterns between experimental conditions. In this context, "clustering" refers to the spatial aggregation of a marker with itself—that is, the extent to which a marker tends to form clusters or aggregates within cells rather than being uniformly distributed.

The analysis is performed separately for each cell type, comparing clustering patterns between target and reference conditions. A statistical test (typically the Wilcoxon rank-sum test) is used to assess whether the distribution of clustering scores (log2 ratio of observed versus expected self-proximity) differs significantly between conditions. Multiple testing correction is applied using FDR to control for the number of statistical tests performed across markers and cell types.

```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_differential_proximity

differential_clustering_analysis <-
    names(markers_to_test) |>
    map(function(x) {
        tryCatch(
            {
                # Store markers in a variable to ensure proper evaluation in lazy query context
                # Convert to character vector to ensure proper SQL translation
                markers_vec <- as.character(markers_to_test[[x]])
                proximity_data <- ProximityScores(subset(pg_data, cell_annotation == x),
                    lazy = TRUE,
                    meta_data_columns = c("sample_alias", "condition")
                ) |>
                    filter(marker_1 == marker_2) |>
                    filter(marker_1 %in% markers_vec) |>
                    filter(marker_2 %in% markers_vec) |>
                    collect()

                res <- DifferentialProximityAnalysis(proximity_data,
                    contrast_column = "condition",
                    reference = "resting",
                    proximity_metric = "log2_ratio",
                    min_n_obs = 5
                )
                res$group <- x
                return(res)
            },
            error = function(e) {
                message(paste("Error for cell type:", x, ":", e))
                return(NULL)
            }
        )
    }) |>
    bind_rows() |>
    mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(p, method = "fdr")) # recalculate the adjusted p values after multiple testing correction for all comparisons, not only per cell type

dir.create(here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/clustering"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Save the differential abundance analysis results to a file
saveRDS(differential_clustering_analysis, here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/clustering/differential_clustering_analysis.rds"))

# Save the differential abundance analysis results to a csv file
write.csv(differential_clustering_analysis, here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/clustering/differential_clustering_analysis.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

```


### 5.3.1. Summarizing plots

Here we create two types of summarizing plots for the differential clustering analysis results:

1. **Volcano plot**: Shows the relationship between effect size (log fold change) and statistical significance (-log10 adjusted p-value) for each marker, faceted by cell type. This helps identify markers that are both significantly different and have large effect sizes.

2. **Heatmap**: Displays significant markers (adjusted p-value < 0.05) across all cell types, with colors representing the effect size. This provides an overview of which markers are differentially clustered in which cell types.

```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_differential_clustering_plots

# Determine the effect size column name
effect_size_col <- "diff_median"

# Determine the feature/marker column name
feature_col <- "marker_1"

# Extract target and reference condition names from the dataframe
# Handle multiple target conditions by identifying all comparisons
reference_condition <- "resting" # Known from DifferentialProximityAnalysis call

# Determine target conditions - check if dataframe has target/contrast columns
if ("target" %in% colnames(differential_clustering_analysis)) {
    # If target column exists, get all unique targets
    target_conditions <- unique(differential_clustering_analysis$target)
    target_conditions <- target_conditions[!is.na(target_conditions)]
} else {
    stop("Could not find target column. Available columns: ", paste(colnames(differential_clustering_analysis), collapse = ", "))
}

# Prepare base data for plotting (add target column if it doesn't exist)
plot_data_base <- differential_clustering_analysis |>
    mutate(
        neg_log10_p_adj = -log10(p_adj),
        significant = p_adj < 0.05,
        effect_size = !!sym(effect_size_col),
        marker = !!sym(feature_col)
    )



# Loop through each target condition and create plots
for (target_condition in target_conditions) {
    # Filter data for this target (if target column exists)
    if ("target" %in% colnames(plot_data_base)) {
        plot_data <- plot_data_base |>
            filter(target == target_condition)
    }

    # Skip if no data for this target
    if (nrow(plot_data) == 0) {
        warning(paste("No data found for target condition:", target_condition))
        next
    }

    # Create directory name based on target and reference
    comparison_dir <- paste0(target_condition, "_vs_", reference_condition)
    base_output_dir <- here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/clustering/differential_clustering_plots", comparison_dir)

    # Plot 1: Volcano plot showing effect size vs significance, faceted by cell type
    # This plot helps identify markers that are both significantly different and have large effect sizes
    # Identify top 5 most extreme effect sizes per cell type for labeling
    top_markers_faceted <- plot_data |>
        group_by(group) |>
        slice_max(order_by = abs(effect_size), n = 5) |>
        ungroup()

    volcano_plot_differential_clustering <- plot_data |>
        ggplot(aes(x = effect_size, y = neg_log10_p_adj, color = effect_size)) +
        geom_point() +
        scale_color_gradient2(
            low = "#0066FFFF",
            mid = "#74D055FF",
            high = "#FF0000FF",
            midpoint = 0
        ) +
        geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
        geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
        geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), col = "grey") +
        coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.1 * min(plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE), 1.1 * max(plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE))) +
        facet_wrap(~group, scales = "free") +
        geom_text(
            data = top_markers_faceted,
            aes(label = marker),
            hjust = -0.2,
            vjust = 0
        ) +
        theme_bw() +
        labs(
            title = paste("Differential clustering analysis: Volcano plot (", target_condition, " vs ", reference_condition, ")", sep = ""),
            subtitle = "Effect size vs statistical significance by cell type",
            x = paste("Effect size (", effect_size_col, ")", sep = ""),
            y = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
            color = "Effect size"
        ) +
        theme(
            strip.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue", color = "black"),
            panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
            panel.grid.major = element_blank()
        )

    # Save faceted volcano plot in the comparison directory
    save_plot(
        filename = file.path(base_output_dir, "differential_clustering_volcano"),
        plot = volcano_plot_differential_clustering,
        width = 12,
        height = 8,
        create_dir = TRUE
    )

    # Plot 2: Non-faceted volcano plots per cell type
    # Create individual folders per cell type and save non-faceted volcano plots for each cell type
    # Get unique cell types (groups)
    unique_cell_types <- unique(plot_data$group)

    # For each cell type, create a non-faceted volcano plot
    for (cell_type in unique_cell_types) {
        # Create directory for this cell type
        cell_type_dir <- file.path(base_output_dir, cell_type)
        dir.create(cell_type_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

        # Filter data for this cell type
        cell_type_plot_data <- plot_data |>
            filter(group == cell_type)

        # Create non-faceted volcano plot for this cell type
        # Identify top 5 most extreme effect sizes for labeling
        top_markers_cell_type <- cell_type_plot_data |>
            slice_max(order_by = abs(effect_size), n = 5)

        volcano_plot_cell_type <- cell_type_plot_data |>
            ggplot(aes(x = effect_size, y = neg_log10_p_adj, color = effect_size)) +
            geom_point() +
            scale_color_gradient2(
                low = "#0066FFFF",
                mid = "#74D055FF",
                high = "#FF0000FF",
                midpoint = 0
            ) +
            geom_hline(yintercept = 0) +
            geom_vline(xintercept = 0) +
            geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), col = "grey") +
            coord_cartesian(xlim = c(1.1 * min(cell_type_plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE), 1.1 * max(cell_type_plot_data$effect_size, na.rm = TRUE))) +
            geom_text(
                data = top_markers_cell_type,
                aes(label = marker),
                hjust = -0.2,
                vjust = 0
            ) +
            theme_bw() +
            labs(
                title = paste("Differential clustering analysis: Volcano plot", sep = ""),
                subtitle = paste("Cell type: ", cell_type, " (", target_condition, " vs ", reference_condition, ")", sep = ""),
                x = paste("Effect size (", effect_size_col, ")", sep = ""),
                y = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
                color = "Effect size"
            ) +
            theme(
                plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
                plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
                panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
                panel.grid.major = element_blank()
            )

        # Save non-faceted volcano plot
        save_plot(
            filename = file.path(cell_type_dir, "volcano_plot"),
            plot = volcano_plot_cell_type,
            width = 8,
            height = 6,
            create_dir = FALSE # Directory already created
        )
    }

    # Plot 3: Heatmap showing significant markers across cell types
    # This provides an overview of which markers are differentially clustered in which cell types
    # Filter for significant markers and prepare data for heatmap
    significant_markers <- plot_data |>
        filter(significant) |>
        select(marker, group, effect_size) |>
        # Pivot to wide format for heatmap
        pivot_wider(names_from = group, values_from = effect_size, values_fill = 0) |>
        column_to_rownames("marker") |>
        as.matrix()

    # Only create heatmap if there are significant markers
    if (nrow(significant_markers) > 0 && ncol(significant_markers) > 0) {
        # Determine color scale breaks based on robust data range (use 1st and 99th percentiles to reduce outlier influence)
        effect_vals <- as.numeric(significant_markers)
        effect_quantiles <- quantile(effect_vals, probs = c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)
        max_abs_effect <- max(abs(effect_quantiles))
        breaks_range <- seq(-max_abs_effect, max_abs_effect, length.out = 101)

        heatmap_differential_clustering <- significant_markers |>
            pheatmap(
                color = PixelgenGradient(100, "BluesCherry"),
                breaks = breaks_range,
                cellwidth = 12,
                cellheight = 8,
                cluster_rows = TRUE,
                cluster_cols = TRUE,
                heatmap_legend_param = list(title = "Effect size"),
                fontsize = 8,
                fontsize_row = 6,
                fontsize_col = 8
            ) |>
            as.ggplot()

        save_plot(
            filename = file.path(base_output_dir, "differential_clustering_heatmap"),
            plot = heatmap_differential_clustering,
            width = 10,
            height = max(6, nrow(significant_markers) * 0.3),
            create_dir = TRUE
        )
    } else {
        warning(paste("No significant markers found for heatmap visualization (p_adj < 0.05) for", target_condition, "vs", reference_condition))
    }
}
```

## 5.4. Differential Colocalization Analysis

Differential colocalization analysis identifies markers that exhibit statistically significant differences in spatial proximity patterns between experimental conditions. The analysis is performed separately for each cell type, comparing colocalization patterns between target and reference conditions. A statistical test is used to assess whether the distribution of colocalization scores (log2 ratio of observed versus expected proximity) differs significantly between conditions. Multiple testing correction is applied using FDR to control for the number of statistical tests performed across markers and cell types.


```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_differential_colocalization


differential_colocalization_analysis <-
    names(markers_to_test) |>
    map(function(x) {
        tryCatch(
            {
                # Store markers in a variable to ensure proper evaluation in lazy query context
                # Convert to character vector to ensure proper SQL translation
                markers_vec <- as.character(markers_to_test[[x]])
                proximity_data <- ProximityScores(subset(pg_data, cell_annotation == x),
                    lazy = TRUE,
                    meta_data_columns = c("sample_alias", "condition")
                ) |>
                    filter(marker_1 %in% markers_vec) |>
                    filter(marker_2 %in% markers_vec) |>
                    collect()

                res <- DifferentialProximityAnalysis(proximity_data,
                    contrast_column = "condition",
                    reference = "resting",
                    proximity_metric = "log2_ratio",
                    min_n_obs = 5
                )
                res$group <- x
                return(res)
            },
            error = function(e) {
                message(paste("Error for cell type:", x, ":", e))
                return(NULL)
            }
        )
    }) |>
    bind_rows() |>
    mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(p, method = "fdr")) # recalculate the adjusted p values after multiple testing correction for all comparisons, not only per cell type

dir.create(here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/colocalization"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Save the differential abundance analysis results to a file
saveRDS(differential_colocalization_analysis, here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/colocalization/differential_colocalization_analysis.rds"))

# Save the differential abundance analysis results to a csv file
write.csv(differential_colocalization_analysis, here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/colocalization/differential_colocalization_analysis.csv"), row.names = FALSE)


```

### 5.4.1. Summarizing plots

Here we create summarizing plots for the differential colocalization analysis results:

1. **Colocalization Heatmap**: Displays the difference in medians for marker pairs, with dot size representing statistical significance (-log10 adjusted p-value). This provides an overview of which marker pairs are differentially colocalized for each cell type and condition comparison.

```{r}
#| label: statistical_testing_differential_colocalization_plot

# Extract target and reference condition names from the dataframe
reference_condition <- "resting" # Known from DifferentialProximityAnalysis call

# Determine target conditions - check if dataframe has target/contrast columns
if ("target" %in% colnames(differential_colocalization_analysis)) {
    # If target column exists, get all unique targets
    target_conditions <- unique(differential_colocalization_analysis$target)
    target_conditions <- target_conditions[!is.na(target_conditions)]
} else {
    stop("Could not find target column. Available columns: ", paste(colnames(differential_colocalization_analysis), collapse = ", "))
}

# Get unique cell types (groups)
unique_cell_types <- unique(differential_colocalization_analysis$group)
unique_cell_types <- unique_cell_types[!is.na(unique_cell_types)]

# Loop through each target condition and cell type combination
for (target_condition in target_conditions) {
    # Create directory name based on target and reference
    comparison_dir <- paste0(target_condition, "_vs_", reference_condition)
    base_output_dir <- here::here("results/05_statistical_testing/colocalization/differential_colocalization_plots", comparison_dir)

    for (cell_type in unique_cell_types) {
        # Create directory for this cell type
        cell_type_dir <- file.path(base_output_dir, cell_type)
        dir.create(cell_type_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

        # Filter data for this target and cell type
        # Set diff_median to 0 for non-significant results (p_adj >= 0.001) to focus on highly significant changes
        plot_data <- differential_colocalization_analysis %>%
            filter(group == cell_type & target == target_condition) %>%
            mutate(diff_median = case_when(
                p_adj < 0.001 ~ diff_median,
                TRUE ~ 0
            ))

        # Skip if no data for this combination
        if (nrow(plot_data) == 0) {
            warning(paste("No data found for target condition:", target_condition, "and cell type:", cell_type))
            next
        }

        # Create the colocalization heatmap plot
        # Add title indicating the target and cell type
        colocalization_heatmap <- ColocalizationHeatmap(
            plot_data,
            marker1_col = "marker_1",
            marker2_col = "marker_2",
            value_col = "diff_median",
            type = "dots",
            size_col_transform = \(x) {
                -log10(x)
            }
        ) &
            labs(size = "-log10(p_adj)") &
            scale_size(range = c(1, 5), limits = c(0, max(-log10(plot_data$p_adj)))) &
            labs(fill = "Difference\nin medians") &
            labs(
                title = paste("Differential Colocalization Analysis", sep = ""),
                subtitle = paste("Cell type: ", cell_type, " (", target_condition, " vs ", reference_condition, ")", sep = "")
            )

        # Save the plot
        save_plot(
            filename = file.path(cell_type_dir, "colocalization_heatmap"),
            plot = colocalization_heatmap,
            width = 10,
            height = 8,
            create_dir = FALSE # Directory already created
        )
    }
}
```
